era_context: The early 14th century continues with the Yuan Dynasty in China under Emperor Renzong (r. 1311–1320). In Europe, the Avignon Papacy under Pope John XXII (r. 1316–1334) begins; John XXII was elected in August 1316. The Great Famine (1315–1317) continues to devastate northern Europe. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia.

events.title: Election of Pope John XXII
events.description: Jacques Duèze was elected Pope on 7 August 1316, taking the name John XXII. He was crowned in Avignon, continuing the Avignon Papacy (1309–1377). John XXII was a strong administrator and theologian who would dominate European religious and political affairs for the next 18 years. His election marked the beginning of a papacy characterized by aggressive assertion of papal authority, conflict with secular rulers, and theological disputes (particularly over the nature of poverty and the Beatific Vision).
events.certainty_note: The election of Pope John XXII is precisely dated and well-documented in papal records.

events.title: Continuation of the Great Famine in Northern Europe
events.region: Northern Europe (England, Scotland, Scandinavia, Low Countries)
events.description: The Great Famine that began in 1315 continued through 1316, with severe crop failures and widespread suffering. Food prices remained at crisis levels, and malnutrition and disease continued to claim lives. The famine's effects on population, economy, and social stability were profound. In some regions, mortality rates reached 5–10% of the population.
events.certainty_note: The Great Famine is extensively documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Death of King Louis X of France; Succession Crisis
events.description: King Louis X of France died on 5 June 1316. His death created a succession crisis because his widow, Clementia of Hungary, was pregnant. The question of whether a female could inherit the French throne, or whether the unborn child would be male, created political uncertainty. Louis X's brother, Philip, eventually became regent and then king (Philip V), establishing the principle that females could not inherit the French throne—a decision that would have profound consequences for European history.
key_figures: Philip V of France (King, brother of Louis X)
Clementia of Hungary (widow of Louis X, pregnant at his death)
events.certainty_note: The death of Louis X and the succession crisis are precisely dated and well-documented.

events.title: Birth of John of Bohemia (Jean de Luxembourg)
events.description: John of Bohemia (Jean de Luxembourg), also known as John the Blind, was born in 1296 (not 1316). However, in 1316, John was a young man of about 20 years old and was beginning his political career. He would become one of the most prominent figures of the 14th century, playing a major role in German and Central European politics. (Note: This entry is corrected to reflect actual events of 1316 rather than a birth date.)
events.certainty_note: John of Bohemia's early career in 1316 is less well-documented than his later activities.

events.title: Continued Expansion of the Ottoman Empire
events.description: Under Osman I, the Ottoman beylik continued its territorial expansion in northwestern Anatolia. By 1316, Ottoman forces had consolidated control over significant territories and were establishing themselves as a major regional power. The expansion was gradual but steady, taking advantage of the decline of Byzantine power and the fragmentation of other Turkish states.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion is well-attested, though specific events for 1316 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The succession crisis following Louis X's death is sometimes portrayed as a clear-cut decision to exclude females from the French throne, but the situation was more complex. The principle of female exclusion was not formally established until later, and there was genuine uncertainty about the legitimacy of Philip V's claim. The decision to exclude females was partly pragmatic (concerns about a female ruler) and partly based on selective interpretation of Salic law.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1316 is dominated by Western European sources, particularly French and English chronicles. The election of Pope John XXII is well-documented in papal records. The Great Famine is extensively documented. Chinese sources are well-preserved but less detailed about events in 1316 specifically. Islamic and African sources are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: John XXII's election led to his aggressive assertion of papal authority and conflicts with secular rulers
The succession crisis and exclusion of females from the French throne contributed to tensions that led to the Hundred Years' War
